concepts.title: Module 4.1: The Memory Hierarchy - Why We Paging

content.why_exists: Processors are fast, but memory is slow. To bridge the gap, OS and Hardware create a hierarchy of storage, from registers to disk. Memory management is the art of making the process feel it has unlimited, fast, and private memory.
content.real_world_problem: How can 100 processes share 8GB of RAM without overwriting each other or crashing the system?
content.daily_life_analogy: A library (Disk) vs. a study desk (RAM) vs. your hands (Registers). You can't fit all books on the desk, so you swap them as needed.
content.hardware_story: The CPU doesn't talk to the RAM directly for every instruction. It uses the MMU (Memory Management Unit) to translate addresses on the fly.
content.definition: The OS subsystem responsible for tracking memory usage, allocating space to processes, and managing the mapping between virtual and physical addresses.
content.internals: Relocation registers, Base and Limit registers were the ancestors of modern Paging.
content.performance_tradeoffs: More cache levels reduce latency but increase hardware complexity and cost.

concepts.title: Module 4.2: Virtual Memory - The Great Illusion

content.why_exists: Virtual Memory decouples the address a program uses from the actual physical location in RAM. This allows for isolation, security, and the ability to run programs larger than physical memory.
content.real_world_problem: A process has a bug and writes to address 0x0000. Without Virtual Memory, this could overwrite the kernel's interrupt table.
content.daily_life_analogy: Your house has an address (123 Main St), but the post office uses a routing code. You don't need to know where the sorting facility is to get mail.
content.hardware_story: The MMU intercepts every memory access. If the translation is not in the TLB, it performs a Page Table Walk.
content.definition: A memory management technique that provides an 'idealized' abstraction of the storage resources that are actually available on a given machine.
content.internals: The process sees a contiguous address space (0 to MAX), while the physical pages are scattered across RAM.
content.visual_machine_model: Process A [0x1000] -> MMU -> RAM [0x5000]. Process B [0x1000] -> MMU -> RAM [0x9000].

content.why_exists: To solve external fragmentation, we divide physical memory into fixed-size 'Frames' and logical memory into 'Pages'. Any page can fit into any frame.
content.real_world_problem: Old systems used 'Contiguous Allocation', which left holes in memory too small for new processes. Paging eliminates this entirely.
content.daily_life_analogy: A loose-leaf notebook. You can put any page in any slot, and the index (Page Table) tells you where to find Section 1.
content.formula: Physical Address = Frame Number \times Page Size + Offset
content.internals: Page Tables are stored in RAM. The CR3 register (on x86) points to the current process's page table.
content.worked_example: Page Size: 4KB. Virtual Address: 0x1005. VPN = 1, Offset = 5. If VPN 1 maps to Frame 7, Physical Address = 7 * 4096 + 5 = 0x7005.

content.why_exists: Looking up the page table in RAM for every instruction would slow down the CPU by 50%. The TLB caches these translations in hardware.
content.real_world_problem: Context switches are expensive because they often require flushing the TLB (or using ASIDs).
content.daily_life_analogy: A speed-dial list on your phone. You don't look at the phone book (Page Table) every time you call a friend.
content.hardware_story: The TLB is an Associative Cache. It compares the VPN against all entries in parallel (one clock cycle).
content.definition: Translation Lookaside Buffer: A high-speed hardware cache used to reduce the time taken to access a user memory location.
content.performance_tradeoffs: Large TLBs are fast but consume significant power and chip area.
